title: M/s Infosys BPM Limited v. Rajesh Kumar Sharma
keyArguments: The employee was terminated for cause following a duly conducted domestic inquiry which established gross misconduct including unauthorized disclosure of confidential client data.

The principles of natural justice were fully complied with as the employee was served a charge sheet, given adequate opportunity to respond, and a fair inquiry was conducted by an independent officer.
The Labor Court had erred in setting aside the termination order without properly appreciating the evidence on record and the findings of the Inquiry Officer.
The employee's contention of victimization was unsubstantiated and contradicted by documentary evidence including access logs and email records.
The standing orders of the company expressly categorize unauthorized disclosure of client data as an act of gross misconduct warranting termination.
judgmentSummary: The Delhi High Court allowed the writ petition filed by M/s Infosys BPM Limited and set aside the award of the Labor Court which had directed reinstatement of the respondent-employee. The High Court held that the domestic inquiry was conducted fairly, the findings of misconduct were supported by cogent evidence, and the Labor Court had exceeded its jurisdiction by re-appreciating evidence as an appellate authority. The termination was upheld as valid and lawful.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
Writ Petition (Civil) No. 4821 of 2022

M/s Infosys BPM Limited ... Petitioner
versus
Rajesh Kumar Sharma & Anr. ... Respondents

Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SUDHIR KUMAR JAIN

JUDGMENT

1. The petitioner-employer has filed the present writ petition under Article 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India challenging the award dated 03rd September, 2021 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court-IV, Karkardooma Courts, Delhi, whereby the termination of the respondent-employee was set aside and reinstatement with full back wages was directed.

2. Brief facts relevant for adjudication are that the respondent No. 1, Rajesh Kumar Sharma, was employed as a Senior Process Associate with the petitioner company since January 2015. In November 2019, the petitioner initiated a domestic inquiry against the respondent on charges of unauthorized forwarding of confidential client data to his personal email account, thereby violating the company's Information Security Policy and the standing orders governing conduct of employees.

3. A charge sheet was duly issued on 12th November, 2019. The respondent submitted his reply on 26th November, 2019. An Inquiry Officer was appointed, and the inquiry proceedings were conducted over five sittings between December 2019 and February 2020. The respondent was represented by a co-worker of his choice throughout the proceedings. The Inquiry Officer, upon examination of access logs, email records, and testimony of witnesses, returned a finding of proved misconduct. Consequent thereto, the petitioner issued an order of dismissal on 15th April, 2020.

4. The respondent raised an industrial dispute which was referred to the Labour Court. The Labour Court, vide impugned award, held that the inquiry was vitiated on account of denial of opportunity and that the punishment was disproportionate, directing reinstatement with continuity of service and full back wages.

5. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the Labour Court grossly erred in law by substituting its own findings for those of the Inquiry Officer without identifying any perversity in the inquiry proceedings. Reliance is placed on the judgments of the Supreme Court in Bharat Forge Co. Ltd. v. Uttam Manohar Nakate, (2005) 2 SCC 489, and Maharashtra State Board of Secondary and Higher Secondary Education v. K.S. Gandhi, (1991) 2 SCC 716, to urge that the scope of judicial review in such matters is limited.

6. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondent seeks to support the impugned award contending that the inquiry was a farce and that the petitioner had failed to produce the original email server logs.

7. I have heard the learned counsel for the parties and perused the record.

8. Upon a careful examination of the inquiry proceedings, this Court finds that all requisite procedural safeguards were adhered to. The respondent was served the charge sheet, given adequate time to file his reply, permitted to cross-examine the management witnesses, and the findings recorded by the Inquiry Officer are based on cogent and reliable documentary evidence including server access logs duly certified by the IT department.

9. The Labour Court's finding that the inquiry was vitiated is not supported by any specific infirmity in the proceedings. The Labour Court has, in effect, conducted a de novo appreciation of evidence which falls outside its permissible jurisdiction. The settled legal position, as reiterated in Disciplinary Authority-cum-Regional Manager v. Nikunja Bihari Patnaik, (1996) 9 SCC 69, is that courts exercising supervisory jurisdiction cannot interfere with findings of fact unless they are perverse or based on no evidence.

10. The misconduct of unauthorized disclosure of confidential client data is a serious breach going to the root of the employment relationship in the IT and BPO sector, and the punishment of dismissal cannot be said to be shockingly disproportionate.

11. In view of the foregoing, the impugned award dated 03rd September, 2021 is hereby set aside. The writ petition is allowed. No order as to costs.

(SUDHIR KUMAR JAIN)
JUDGE
MARCH 14, 2023
